original_abstract:
Absract:

The kinetics of the simplest Criegee intermediate, CH$_2$OO, were investigated in reactions with SO$_2$, NO$_2$, NO, H$_2$O, and CH$_3$CHO at 295 K and over an extended pressure range of 25â€“450 Torr. CH$_2$OO was generated via the flash photolysis of CH$_2$I$_2$/O$_2$ mixtures, with reaction progress monitored through the laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) detection of the HCHO product, supplemented by direct photoionisation mass spectrometry (PIMS) for the SO$_2$ system at low pressure. The rate coefficients for the reactions with SO$_2$ and NO$_2$ were determined to be $(3.42 \pm 0.42) \times 10^{-11}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$ and $(1.5 \pm 0.5) \times 10^{-12}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$, respectively. Both values remained independent of pressure across the studied ranges, suggesting that these species act as efficient atmospheric sinks for CH$_2$OO. 

In contrast, the reaction with CH$_3$CHO exhibited distinct pressure dependence; while the rate coefficient increased with pressure, the yield of HCHO decreased, indicating the collisional stabilization of a secondary ozonide at higher pressures. For the reactions with NO and H$_2$O, only upper limits could be established at $2 \times 10^{-13}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$ and $9 \times 10^{-17}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$, respectively. Notably, the upper limit for the CH$_2$OO + H$_2$O reaction is significantly lower than previously reported values. These findings suggest that the atmospheric impact of Criegee intermediates on SO$_2$ oxidation and sulfate aerosol formation may be more pronounced than currently estimated in many models, due to the diminished competitive role of water vapor.
humanized_abstract:
Absract:

The kinetics of the simplest Criegee biradical, CH2OO, were studied in detail in reactions with SO2, NO2, NO, H2O and CH3CHO at 295 K and over a large pressure range of 25â€“450 Torr. CH2OO was produced by flash photolysis of mixtures of CH2I2 and O2 and the evolution of the reaction mixture was observed by laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) detection of the HCHO product, which was unambiguously identified, along with direct photoionisation mass spectrometry (PIMS) detection at low pressure in the SO2 system. The rate coefficients for the reactions of CH2OO with SO2 and NO2 were determined to be (3.42 Â± 0.42) Ă— 10âˆ’11 cm3 sâˆ’1 and (1.5 Â± 0.5) Ă— 10âˆ’12 cm3 sâˆ’1, respectively, and were found to be pressure invariant. The high values of these rate coefficients suggest that both SO2 and NO2NO2 act as effective sink species for CH2OO in the atmosphere.

Reactions of CH3OO with SO2 proceed in a similar fashion to that reported previously for ClOO, with the exception that the CH3CHO reaction displays a distinctly different pressure behaviour: while the rate coefficient increases with pressure, the HCHO yield falls at higher pressures due to collisional stabilization of secondary ozonide. For the NO and H2O reactions only upper limits could be determined, amounting to 2 Ă— 10âˆ’13 cm3 sâˆ’1 and 9 Ă— 10âˆ’17 cm3 sâˆ’1, respectively, with the upper limit for the CH2OO + H2O reaction being a factor of about 200 lower than the recommended rate coefficient. Consequently, the competitive role of water vapour appears to be greatly diminished and thus the atmospheric impact of Criegee intermediates on SO2 oxidation with the associated sulfate aerosol formation is most likely larger than generally predicted in atmospheric models.

document.input:
Absract:

The kinetics of the simplest Criegee intermediate, CH$_2$OO, were investigated in reactions with SO$_2$, NO$_2$, NO, H$_2$O, and CH$_3$CHO at 295 K and over an extended pressure range of 25â€“450 Torr. CH$_2$OO was generated via the flash photolysis of CH$_2$I$_2$/O$_2$ mixtures, with reaction progress monitored through the laser-induced fluorescence (LIF) detection of the HCHO product, supplemented by direct photoionisation mass spectrometry (PIMS) for the SO$_2$ system at low pressure. The rate coefficients for the reactions with SO$_2$ and NO$_2$ were determined to be $(3.42 \pm 0.42) \times 10^{-11}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$ and $(1.5 \pm 0.5) \times 10^{-12}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$, respectively. Both values remained independent of pressure across the studied ranges, suggesting that these species act as efficient atmospheric sinks for CH$_2$OO. 

In contrast, the reaction with CH$_3$CHO exhibited distinct pressure dependence; while the rate coefficient increased with pressure, the yield of HCHO decreased, indicating the collisional stabilization of a secondary ozonide at higher pressures. For the reactions with NO and H$_2$O, only upper limits could be established at $2 \times 10^{-13}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$ and $9 \times 10^{-17}$ cm$^3$ s$^{-1}$, respectively. Notably, the upper limit for the CH$_2$OO + H$_2$O reaction is significantly lower than previously reported values. These findings suggest that the atmospheric impact of Criegee intermediates on SO$_2$ oxidation and sulfate aerosol formation may be more pronounced than currently estimated in many models, due to the diminished competitive role of water vapor.
